text: What is the result of executing the following code snippet?
explanation: myScore is 7, which matches case score1, score2, 7 on line 44 — so "good" is printed. Pattern matching cases are evaluated in order but constant cases take priority.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 130–136

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: The loop adds i to x for i = 0, 1, 2. When i == 3, break exits the loop. x = 0 + 1 + 2 = 3.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 116–118

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: continue skips the rest of the loop body for i == 3. x accumulates 0 + 1 + 2 + 4 = 7 (skipping 3).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 118–120

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: A do-while loop always executes the body at least once. It prints 0, then 1, then 2. After printing 2, i becomes 3 and the condition i < 3 is false, so the loop exits. Output: '0 1 2 '.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 108–110

text: Which of the following switch expression/statement rules are correct in Java 21? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: A switch expression must be exhaustive (cover all possible values).
The yield keyword is used to return a value from a switch expression block.
A traditional switch statement requires a break to prevent fall-through.
Arrow case labels (->) allow fall-through just like colon labels.
A switch expression can use both arrow (->) and colon (:) cases in the same switch.

explanation: Switch expressions must cover all possible selector values (exhaustiveness). yield returns a value from a block in a switch expression. Traditional switch with colon labels falls through unless break is used. Arrow labels do NOT fall through. You cannot mix arrow and colon labels in the same switch.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 126–134

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: continue outer jumps to the next iteration of the outer loop when j == 1. For each i, only j = 0 is printed before continue outer fires. Output: '00 10 20 '.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 120–122

text: What is the output of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error: pattern variable s cannot be used in &&

explanation: Pattern matching instanceof (Java 16+) binds s if obj is a String. The && operator short-circuits, and s is in scope after the instanceof pattern match in the same boolean expression. "Hello" has length 5 > 3, so the if branch executes.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 136–140

text: How many times is 'hello' printed by the following code?
explanation: count++ is post-increment: the condition uses the current value of count before incrementing. Iterations: count=0 (<3, print, count becomes 1), count=1 (<3, print, count becomes 2), count=2 (<3, print, count becomes 3), count=3 (not <3, exit). Printed 3 times.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 106–108

text: What is printed by the following traditional switch statement?
explanation: The switch matches case 2. Because there is no break after case 2, execution falls through to case 3 and prints 'Wed '. The break after case 3 stops execution. Output: 'Tue Wed '.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 122–126

text: Which of the following can be used as the selector expression in a switch statement or expression in Java 21? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Switch supports: byte, short, char, int (and their wrappers), String, and enum types. long, float, double, boolean, and long wrappers are NOT valid switch selector types.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 122–124

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: The enhanced for loop iterates over each element. Even numbers are 2 and 4. sum = 2 + 4 = 6.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 112–115

text: What is the result of the following code?

options.text: Compilation error: yield is not allowed with arrow cases.

explanation: Arrow cases with a block require yield to produce a value. The block yields s.toLowerCase() which is "beta". Arrow cases with a single expression do not need yield. Both forms are valid.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 129–131

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: The dangling else attaches to the nearest if — the inner 'if (i > 8)'. Since i = 10 > 8 is true, 'A' is printed and the else is skipped. The outer if condition (i > 5) is also true, so execution reaches the inner if.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 101–103

text: Which of the following statements about pattern matching in switch (Java 21) are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: A guarded pattern uses the 'when' keyword to add a condition.
A null case must always be listed last.
A default case is required when using type patterns even if all types are covered.
Constant cases are checked before type patterns.
Pattern variables declared in a case are scoped to that case arm.

explanation: Guarded patterns use 'when'. The null case can appear anywhere (not necessarily last). When a sealed class hierarchy is fully covered by patterns, a default is not required. Constant cases take precedence over type patterns. Pattern variables are scoped to their case arm.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 3 – Making Decisions, pp. 136–142
